title: What Law Did I Violate? The Problem of Invisible Law
excerpt: When a city creates a local traffic rule that does not exist as statewide law, the question is not only whether the ordinance exists. The deeper question is whether the public had fair notice before punishment attached.
author: Ting Luo
body: A person can drive across Montana for hours under one basic expectation: the rules of the road should be visible, knowable, and traceable. Speed limits are posted. Stop signs are posted. Lane markings are painted. A driver may not know every statute by number, but the legal command is usually made visible before punishment attaches.
That expectation becomes more complicated when a city creates a local traffic rule that does not exist as a statewide rule.

Consider local restrictions on handheld phone or electronic communication device use while driving. A driver may enter Montana knowing that state law does not function like California law. Then that driver crosses into a city such as Bozeman or Missoula, where a municipal ordinance may impose a local restriction. The legal boundary changes, but the physical road looks exactly the same.

Montana has not simply overlooked this issue. In 2025, Senate Bill 359 proposed a statewide ban on handheld device use while driving. It passed the Montana Senate, moved through the Montana House, and died in process. A failed bill is not an affirmative command — it does not prove the legislature wanted all phone use while driving to remain legal forever. But it means the proposed statewide rule did not become statewide law.

This sharpens the authority problem. If no statewide prohibition exists, but a city enforces a local ordinance against the same conduct, which authority controls the individual? The answer cannot simply be “government.” Government is not one undifferentiated voice. A state legislature, a municipal council, a police department, and an individual officer do not all create law in the same way. A policy may guide an agency; a safety concern may explain enforcement priorities. But neither can substitute for law.

If the city has a valid ordinance, it should say so. If the ordinance rests on Montana’s broad municipal authority, it should trace that authority explicitly — especially after a statewide bill covering the same conduct failed to become law. The question is not whether government has authority. It does. The question is which layer created the legal duty, whether that layer had the power to do so, and whether the duty was visible enough before punishment attached.
This raises a basic rule-of-law question: What law did I violate?

That question is not a technicality. It is the first question any government should be prepared to answer before it punishes a person. A policy is not enough. A department preference is not enough. A general safety concern is not enough. A police officer’s statement that “this is illegal here” is not enough by itself. The government must be able to identify the actual legal authority: the ordinance, the statutory authorization, the jurisdictional boundary, and the conduct prohibited.
**Tracing the Authority Chain**

Montana law does give cities and towns broad ordinance power. That is not the dispute. In fact, that broad power is the starting point of the problem. What follows is the authority chain that a city would need to trace in order to show that a local traffic ordinance rests on valid legal ground.

Article XI, Section 4 of the Montana Constitution gives incorporated cities and towns the powers of municipal corporations, along with legislative, administrative, and other powers provided or implied by law. It also provides that those powers shall be liberally construed.

MCA § 7-5-4101 then gives a city or town council the power to make and pass ordinances, bylaws, orders, and resolutions for the government or management of municipal affairs, for the execution of powers vested in the municipal body, and for carrying into effect the provisions of Title 7, so long as those enactments are not repugnant to the United States Constitution, the Montana Constitution, or Montana law.

In the traffic context, MCA § 61-8-103 creates the basic statewide-uniformity rule: Montana traffic provisions apply uniformly throughout the state, and local authorities may not enact or enforce ordinances that conflict with the state traffic chapter unless expressly authorized. But the same statute also preserves local power — local authorities may adopt additional traffic regulations that do not conflict with state traffic law.
MCA § 7-5-4201 adds the formal ordinance requirement. Municipal ordinances must be passed by the council, approved by the mayor, and recorded in a book kept by the clerk, called “The Ordinance Book.”

So yes, a Montana city may have authority to enact a local traffic ordinance. That power is not the dispute. But that only answers the first question. It answers whether the city may have power to make the rule. It does not answer whether the city may fairly punish an ordinary person who had no realistic notice that the rule existed.

The dispute is what the public actually understands about the rule acting on them. Most people learn the behavioral expectation quickly enough — get pulled over once, hear that a friend was ticketed, and you learn not to use your phone while driving in this city. Compliance happens. But compliance is not the same as understanding.

The person who puts their phone down may have no idea whether they are obeying a state statute, a municipal ordinance, a department policy, or an officer’s personal interpretation. These are legally different instruments with different sources of authority and different paths of challenge. But to the ordinary person, they all look the same: a police officer said it was illegal, and I paid the fine.

The government knows which layer of authority it used. The public does not. And the people who enforce the law — whether they operate in a legislative, judicial, or executive capacity — understand this asymmetry better than anyone. The gap between what government knows about its own authority and what the public knows is not an accident. It is a structural advantage. The less the public understands about the nature of the rule, the harder it is to question. The harder it is to question, the less it needs to justify itself.

A person who wants to challenge the rule does not even know what they are challenging — a statute, an ordinance, or something that may not have the force of law at all. And that is not a failure of the individual. It is a failure of the system to make its own authority legible.
**The Federal Due Process Limit**

The Fourteenth Amendment says that no state may deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law. Due process includes a deeper principle: before government turns law into penalty, the legal duty must be knowable enough for a person to conform their conduct.

Lambert v. California (1957) makes that principle concrete. In that case, a Los Angeles municipal ordinance required any person convicted of a felony to register with the city within five days of entering it. The defendant, a longtime Los Angeles resident, was convicted under the ordinance despite having no knowledge of the registration duty. The United States Supreme Court reversed. The Court held that applying a municipal ordinance to impose a penalty on a person who had no actual knowledge of the duty, and where there was no showing of the probability of such knowledge, violated the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
That is the legal tension this problem exposes. Montana gives cities broad ordinance power. Federal due process limits how hidden local law may be used against people.

A city may be able to pass a local traffic ordinance. It may be able to record it properly. It may even be able to show that the ordinance does not conflict with Montana traffic law. But if the city then uses that ordinance to punish a passing driver who does not live in the city, vote in the city, follow city council meetings, read city websites, or know where the city boundary begins, the question is no longer only one of municipal authority. The question becomes notice.

Was the rule visible? Was the city boundary clear? Was the legal duty meaningfully communicated? Was there actual knowledge — or at least a probability of knowledge? Or did the government create a local legal duty, preserve it in municipal paperwork, and then treat a passerby as though they should have known it all along?
**Formal Legality and Lived Legality**
This is where it helps to draw a distinction that the legal system rarely makes explicit.
Formal legality asks whether a rule exists somewhere — whether it was properly enacted, recorded, and authorized.
Lived legality asks whether the rule was accessible enough to actually guide conduct — whether a person could have discovered it before being punished for violating it.

A healthy legal system needs both. A hidden ordinance may satisfy a filing requirement, but it fails the civic function of law if people cannot reasonably discover it before they are punished. Public availability is not the same as practical visibility. A rule can be technically public and functionally invisible. The question is not whether a lawyer can find the ordinance after the fact. The question is whether an ordinary person had a fair opportunity to know the rule before the government used it against them.

This does not mean local governments should be powerless. Local conditions matter. Cities may have legitimate reasons to regulate traffic more strictly than the state baseline — dense downtown areas, university zones, pedestrian corridors, places with high crash risk. Local self-government can be a strength. A city should be able to respond to local safety problems.

But local authority comes with local responsibility. If a city chooses to create a local-only traffic prohibition, it should make that prohibition visible. Not buried. Not technically available somewhere on a municipal website. Not discoverable only after a citation. Visible.

At minimum, a driver should be able to understand three things before being punished: what conduct is prohibited, where the prohibition begins and ends, and what legal authority creates the prohibition. The greater the difference between the statewide baseline and the local command, the stronger the city’s notice obligation should be.
Without meaningful notice, enforcement becomes less like law and more like a trap.
**The Deeper Problem**
But even the notice question, as important as it is, does not reach the deepest layer of the problem.

The ordinary person does not merely lack notice of a specific rule. The ordinary person lacks the conceptual vocabulary to understand what kind of rule is being applied. Most people do not know the difference between a statute and an ordinance. They do not know that a municipal ordinance derives its authority from a state constitutional provision and a chain of enabling statutes. They do not know that a city’s power to regulate traffic is preserved by the same statute that requires statewide uniformity. They do not know that “The Ordinance Book” is a formal legal object. They do not know where state law ends and local law begins — and they have never been taught.

The legal system does not require the government to teach any of this. The presumption is that the law is knowable, and that ignorance of the law is no excuse. But that presumption was built for a world in which legal duties were fewer, more visible, and more uniform. It becomes increasingly fictional in a layered system where the same conduct can be legal on one side of a city boundary and illegal on the other, where the source of the prohibition is an ordinance most residents have never heard of, and where the authority chain that validates that ordinance requires constitutional and statutory analysis to reconstruct.
The question, then, is not just was the rule visible?

The question is: if the public cannot distinguish between a law and an ordinance, and the government has never been obligated to teach that distinction, then on what basis does the government presume that the individual understood the nature of the legal duty before being punished for violating it?

This is not a question about one traffic ticket. It is a question about the relationship between legal complexity and democratic legitimacy. A legal system that multiplies layers of authority, distributes rulemaking power across overlapping jurisdictions, and then holds individuals responsible for navigating that complexity without guidance is not a system that takes seriously the principle that law should be knowable before it is enforceable.

The government should not be afraid of a simple question. And in a system that calls itself lawful, the government should be required to answer it — not just with a citation to an ordinance book, but with a legible account of the authority, the boundary, the duty, and the notice that preceded the punishment.
Formal legality may keep the system internally consistent. But only lived legality keeps it legitimate.
